title: The New Cup

jokes.text: The cup roster debate — Jemaine has created a formal rotating schedule for a single shared cup
jokes.setup: Bret bought a new cup because they only have one, implying extreme poverty and inconvenience
jokes.punchline: Jemaine reveals there is already a formal 'cup roster' — a bureaucratic schedule governing who gets to use the one cup at what hours
jokes.explanation: The cup roster is a perfectly calibrated absurdist premise for this show: a man so controlling and so poor that he has formalized bureaucratic governance over a single cup. It's completely character-specific to Jemaine's pedantic, rule-bound personality and the show's running theme of extreme New York poverty. The joke earns its laughs through escalating specificity — it's not just a 'schedule,' it's hours-defined slots with a rest period for the cup itself. Exceptional character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's a cup.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What, a new cup?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got a new cup. Do you like it?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We've got a cup.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, we've got one cup. We needed two cups.

jokes.text: And then we give the cup a rest.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has outlined a detailed hourly cup-use schedule as if managing a piece of heavy machinery
jokes.punchline: The cup is given a formal rest period, as though it is a living worker entitled to breaks
jokes.explanation: Four words that are the perfect escalating cap to the cup roster bit. The economy here is exceptional — 'we give the cup a rest' delivered deadpan treats the inanimate object with the same labor-relations courtesy you'd give an employee. It's the peak of the absurdist logic and the most quotable line in the cold open. The understatement of deadpan delivery makes it funnier than any exclamation could.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, do you even pay any attention to the cup roster?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Your cup roster.

jokes.text: It was only $2.79.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been deeply outraged by the purchase of a second cup and the disruption to the roster
jokes.punchline: Bret's defense of the cup is its low price — $2.79 — delivered with earnest innocence as if this settles everything
jokes.explanation: The $2.79 figure is perfectly chosen — low enough to be pathetic, specific enough to be funny, and it plants the seed for the cascading financial disaster to come. Bret's earnest delivery of it as a justification is very on-brand. Functions as both a standalone joke and essential setup for joke #5.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That means I can't drink a cup of tea between 7:00 and 9:00 pm.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, I have it till 9:00 and then you get it for two hours.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Then I use it from 11:00 till 1:00.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You use it from 1:00 till 3:00.

jokes.text: Okay, well, I just need a second to calm down.
jokes.setup: A heated dispute over a $2.79 cup has genuinely distressed Jemaine
jokes.punchline: Jemaine treats the cup disagreement as a serious emotional episode requiring recovery time
jokes.explanation: A sharp character beat — Jemaine's disproportionate reaction to the cup situation is played completely straight. The 'calm down' line lands because it treats a trivial domestic purchase as a genuine emotional crisis, which is entirely consistent with his pedantic, over-serious personality.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: And then we give the cup a rest.

jokes.text: The $2.79 cup causes cascading financial catastrophe: bounced phone check → $30 overdraft fee → bounced gas bill → $60 owed → phone and gas cut off
jokes.setup: Bret's $2.79 cup purchase overdrew the account by exactly $2.79
jokes.punchline: That single cup purchase triggers a cascading chain of financial disaster — overdraft fees, bounced bills, utilities cut off — framed as a logical consequence of a cup
jokes.explanation: This is excellent structural comedy — the $2.79 figure planted earlier now pays off as the precise amount that overdrew the account, and the domino effect of consequences is both realistic (overdraft fees are genuinely predatory) and perfectly absurd in context. The structure is tight: each beat escalates logically. 'Yeah, that's boring, man' as Bret's reaction mid-explanation is a great deadpan interruption that itself earns a laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Okay, well, I just need a second to calm down.

jokes.text: Yeah, that's boring, man.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is carefully explaining the cascading financial consequences of Bret's cup purchase
jokes.punchline: Bret dismisses the explanation of their own financial ruin as 'boring'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Bret. His total disengagement from the practical consequences of his own actions is a core character trait, and 'that's boring, man' delivered mid-crisis is a masterclass in deadpan. Maximum funny per word — four words undercut an entire explanation. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How much was that cup again?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, that's quite interesting, isn't it?

jokes.text: Stink.
jokes.setup: Jemaine finishes explaining that they now owe $60 and their phone and gas are being cut off, all because of the cup
jokes.punchline: Bret's entire response to this financial catastrophe is the single New Zealand slang word 'Stink' — meaning 'that's unfortunate'
jokes.explanation: One word, perfect economy. 'Stink' is New Zealand slang for 'that's bad/unfortunate' and it's the most hilariously understated possible response to being told their utilities are being cut off because of a cup. It's character-specific (distinctly Kiwi), maximally economical, and lands as a genuine laugh line because the gap between the severity of the situation and the mildness of the response is vast. One of the episode's best individual lines.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, but the point is because of your $2.79 spending spree, we now owe $60 and our phone and gas are gonna be cut off.

jokes.text: We got a letter from the electricity company. — What did it say? — Didn't read it.
jokes.setup: They're already having financial problems; Jemaine announces a letter from the electricity company
jokes.punchline: He didn't read it — the news is so likely to be bad that he simply chose not to engage
jokes.explanation: The 'didn't read it' punchline is a great three-beat exchange with a satisfying anti-climax. It captures a very relatable response to bad news (denial/avoidance) played completely straight. Clean structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Stink.

jokes.text: The 'dad guitar' vs 'mum guitar' bit — Bret voices both as domestic archetypes
jokes.setup: Jemaine points out Bret has no guitar; Bret says it won't sound weird with just the bass
jokes.punchline: Bret personifies the bass as a gruff dad and the guitar as a gentle mum, doing voices for both in a domestic dispute scenario involving Murray and the lawn
jokes.explanation: A genuinely inventive bit — anthropomorphizing musical instruments as a married couple with distinct voices and domestic dynamics is original and very Bret-coded. The specific detail of Murray being told to mow the lawn brings in a third character comedically. The voices and the scenario ('I was having a few beers') add layers. Slight economy penalty because it runs a little long, but the originality is high.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Ah. We got a letter from the electricity company.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What did it say?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Didn't read it.

jokes.text: You'd have to be deaf to hear that. [Bret plays air guitar]
jokes.setup: Bret has no guitar and is apparently miming/air-guitaring; Jemaine asks how it sounds
jokes.punchline: Jemaine delivers the logically inverted line: 'you'd have to be deaf to hear that' — meaning it's completely silent, the opposite of the normal idiom
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed wordplay/logic inversion. The standard idiom is 'you'd have to be deaf NOT to hear that' (meaning it's loud). Flipping it to mean total silence is clever, economical, and lands as a proper joke rather than just a description. Highly quotable and the kind of line that gets funnier on rewatch once you catch the inversion.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We got a letter from the electricity company.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What did it say?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Didn't read it.

jokes.text: Murray's newsletter review: 'Conchords crash and burn — by Murray Hewitt' — Murray wrote his own band's scathing review
jokes.setup: Murray reads from the New Zealand Consulate newsletter's music review section about the Conchords' gig
jokes.punchline: The review is scathing ('a musical embarrassment, two stars') and is bylined 'by Murray Hewitt' — their own manager wrote it
jokes.explanation: Murray as a character is defined by his delusional authority and his strange dual role as both advocate and critic of the band. Writing a scathing review of his own clients in an official newsletter — and then presenting it to them as 'what people are saying' — is perfect Murray comedy. The reveal of the byline ('by Murray Hewitt') is the punchline and it lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The bass. It's called a bass.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, I call it the dad guitar, 'cause it's more like a... 'I'm your dad. Hey, Murray, get into the shed and get the mower... do the lawn.'

jokes.text: Oh, good news. The new New Zealand consulate newsletter is out.
jokes.setup: The band has just bombed their gig with no instruments. Murray is their manager.
jokes.punchline: Murray announces the newsletter as 'good news' with genuine enthusiasm, completely tone-deaf to the actual crisis at hand. The comedy is the mismatch between his excitement and the situation.
jokes.explanation: Classic Murray: the newsletter as 'good news' reveals his complete detachment from band priorities. High character integration — this joke only works because of who Murray is.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You need Bret's mum guitar to add the beautiful tones. 'Come on, now. Murray's okay. He's with me. Why you get home so late, Gordon?' 'I was having a few beers.'

jokes.text: Two stars out of 100, not out of five — 'Well, I wish it was, but it's out of 100.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is upset about the two-star review; Murray clarifies it's out of 100, not out of five
jokes.punchline: 'Well, I wish it was' — Murray concedes he wished it were out of five, implying even he knows the rating is brutally low
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured exchange. 'Two stars out of five' is recoverable; 'two out of 100' is crushing. Murray's 'well, I wish it was' is the perfect beat — a reluctant acknowledgment that makes him complicit in the humiliation. Then 'Don't tell people that / Say it's out of five' adds a further layer of Murray's pathetic spin-doctoring. Tight, layered, very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You'd have to be deaf to hear that.

jokes.text: Say it's out of five.
jokes.setup: The two-out-of-100 rating is embarrassing; Murray just suggested pretending it's out of five
jokes.punchline: Murray's entire PR strategy for the band's terrible review is to lie about the scoring system
jokes.explanation: The natural button on the previous joke — Murray's advice as a manager is simply to deceive people about the rating. Perfectly in character. The economy is excellent; it's two additional words that extend the joke without overstaying.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Conchords crash and burn. Doesn't sound too good. By Murray hewitt.
You wrote this?

jokes.text: The Nigerian internet scam — Murray invested the band's emergency fund in an obvious internet scam from 'Nigel Soladu'
jokes.setup: Bret asks about using the emergency band fund to buy back his guitar
jokes.punchline: Murray reveals he's invested the fund in a Nigerian internet scam, but genuinely believes it's legitimate — 'the internet service, one of the trusted things of today's society'
jokes.explanation: The Nigerian email scam as a plot device is not entirely original (it was a well-worn reference point by the late 2000s), but Murray's genuine bewilderment that anyone might doubt its legitimacy is perfectly in character. 'Why would someone want to scam me, Jemaine' is a great line. 'One of the trusted things of today's society' is a wonderfully clunky piece of Murray-speak.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Two stars.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Two stars?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Two stars.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You can't give us two stars, Murray. Two out of five stars?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, I wish it was, but it's out of 100.

jokes.text: Why would someone want to scam me, Jemaine, and on the internet service, one of the trusted things of today's society?
jokes.setup: Jemaine says the Nigerian email is a scam
jokes.punchline: Murray's defense is his own bafflement at why anyone would target him specifically, and his faith in 'the internet service' as inherently trustworthy
jokes.explanation: 'One of the trusted things of today's society' is exactly the kind of clunky, bureaucratic Murray-speak that defines the character. His self-centered framing ('why would someone scam ME') adds another layer of narcissism. Highly quotable in isolation and perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Don't tell people that. Say it's out of five.

jokes.text: The biscuit ordering bureaucracy — getting a biscuit requires filling out a form sent to Wellington, with an ID check, delivery by Tuesday
jokes.setup: Bret asks if there's any food at the consulate
jokes.punchline: Murray produces an elaborate bureaucratic form system for ordering biscuits — requires name, quantity, ID, gets processed in Wellington, delivery by Tuesday
jokes.explanation: Murray's absurd bureaucratic impulse applied to something as trivial as a biscuit request is a gold-standard Murray joke. The specificity of 'sent back to Wellington' and 'you'll have your biscuits by Tuesday' escalates perfectly. The ID requirement is an inspired detail. This is Murray's character joke in microcosm — applying maximum procedure to minimum stakes.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's a friend of mine from nigeria. - Nigel soladu.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: When did you go to nigeria?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't go. That's the best bit.

jokes.text: The Superstraw visual gag — Bret has invented a 'Superstraw' product (multiple straws taped together to drink from far away)
jokes.setup: Murray asks who would want to buy one of those
jokes.punchline: Bret explains the Superstraw — 'mainly people who need to drink from far away' — an absurd product with a deadpan market rationale
jokes.explanation: The Superstraw is a wonderfully Bret invention — low-tech, earnestly conceived, and completely useless. 'Mainly people who need to drink from far away' delivered with total sincerity is the perfect deadpan rationale. Also functions as setup for the later economics joke (#34). Character-specific to Bret's naive entrepreneurial spirit.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Why would someone want to scam me, Jemaine, and on the internet service, one of the trusted things of today's society?

jokes.text: Who's gonna want to buy one of those, Bret? [cut to Mel immediately buying one]
jokes.setup: Jemaine dismisses Bret's 'Superstraw' product (multiple straws bundled together) with open skepticism
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to Mel enthusiastically purchasing one — instant comic refutation of Jemaine's prediction
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom edit structure — the immediate cut refuting the skeptic — but executed with enough confidence and Mel's reliable fan-hysteria character that it lands cleanly. The economy of the visual cut does the work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Just fill out this. Put how many biscuits you want, your name, and you'll have to bring in some I.D. That gets sent back to wellington. We'll have your biscuits by Tuesday.

jokes.text: Wow, that's so romantic... two struggling musicians living in poverty and despair, selling their instruments.
jokes.setup: Bret explains he sold his guitar to pay the bills
jokes.punchline: Mel, their obsessive fan, finds their poverty and desperation genuinely romantic and exciting — reframing their misery as a beautiful narrative
jokes.explanation: A very Mel joke — her obsessive fan perspective transforms their genuine suffering into a romantic narrative. 'Poverty and despair' said with starry-eyed admiration is perfectly wrong. High character integration; only Mel would respond this way.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Mainly people who need to drink from far away.

jokes.text: I know it sounds good, but it's actually pretty dressing.
jokes.setup: Mel has called their situation 'romantic'; Jemaine wants to correct her
jokes.punchline: Jemaine says 'pretty dressing' — apparently meaning 'pretty distressing' but Kiwi-accented/understated to the point of comic deflation; the word 'dressing' also functions as an accidental comic misstatement
jokes.explanation: 'Pretty dressing' reads as either a malapropism (distressing → dressing) or deliberate understatement — either way it's character-specific and funny. The Kiwi deadpan of saying something horrifying in the most mild possible terms is a show signature.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Wow, that's so romantic... two struggling musicians living in poverty and despair, selling their instruments.

jokes.text: Oh, well, I could give you guys some money. — Yeah? — Thanks, Mel. — I mean, you would have to earn it, of course. I mean, I wouldn't insult you by giving you charity. — Do you guys... do massage?
jokes.setup: Mel offers to give them money; the guys are grateful; she adds they'd have to earn it
jokes.punchline: Her 'earning it' proposition turns out to be: do you guys do massage? — delivered with uncomfortable fan-obsessive energy
jokes.explanation: A well-structured cringe setup: the 'earn it' pivot is perfectly timed, and 'do you guys... do massage?' lands as both an awkward request and the beginning of an uncomfortable scene. The pause before 'do massage' is key timing. Mel's character is used perfectly here.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I know it sounds good, but it's actually pretty dressing.

jokes.text: The escalating finger-pointing over who does massage — both Bret and Jemaine simultaneously deny it while attributing it to the other
jokes.setup: Mel asks if they do massage; both immediately try to dodge and assign it to the other
jokes.punchline: Overlapping denials and counter-accusations ('Bret does.' 'No.' 'I don't, but Bret does.' 'Bret's always massaging.' 'Bret's a real good masseuse.') escalate hilariously as Jemaine confidently fabricates Bret's expertise
jokes.explanation: The overlapping, escalating attribution scene is a classic ensemble cringe comedy technique, executed with great timing. Jemaine confidently asserting that 'Bret's always massaging' as if it's established fact is particularly funny. The rhythm of denial/counter-accusation is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Bret does.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You do.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Bret does.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't, but Bret does.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He does.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Jemaine's the one for that.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Bret always does that.

jokes.text: The massage scene — Bret massages Mel so gently she can't feel it; 'I'm just doing it very gently to begin with'
jokes.setup: Bret has agreed to give Mel a massage to earn money; he clearly doesn't want to be there
jokes.punchline: Bret's massage is so tentative Mel can't feel it at all; his explanation — 'I'm just doing it very gently to begin with' — implies a process that will never escalate
jokes.explanation: The discomfort comedy here comes from Bret's complete unwillingness to actually commit to the task while technically complying. 'I'm just doing it very gently to begin with' is a great deadpan excuse. The scene's cringe value is high because of the power dynamics (obsessive fan, reluctant musician, husband watching).

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm just doing it very gently to begin with.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Are you... I can't really feel it. I wanna feel it.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm fine with it. Unless I start feeling something, I guess I'm not gonna be able to pay you $30.
jokes.setup: Mel assures Bret that her husband Doug is fine with the massage
jokes.punchline: Doug, who has apparently been watching this entire time, reveals he'll only pay if the massage becomes actually sensual — stated completely flatly
jokes.explanation: A perfectly timed reveal — Doug being present and monitoring the massage for threshold-level sensation is both unexpected and horrifying in the best cringe way. His flat, contractual delivery ('I'm not gonna be able to pay you $30') treats it as a mundane transaction.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Should I lay down? No no, this is good. Maybe I should take off some clothes... no no, that's good.

jokes.text: You're like a samurai. Like a massage samurai.
jokes.setup: Mel is thrilled by what is apparently now an effective massage
jokes.punchline: 'Massage samurai' — an absurd compliment that is both enthusiastic and meaningless, delivered with Mel's characteristic over-the-top fan energy
jokes.explanation: Funny in context and very Mel — the specificity of 'samurai' as a modifier for 'massage' is absurd. Not a top-tier joke but a solid character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You're like a samurai, like a massage samurai.

jokes.text: I'm just patting her.
jokes.setup: Mel is in ecstasy over the 'massage samurai'; the audience assumes he must be doing something impressive
jokes.punchline: Bret's deadpan confession: 'I'm just patting her' — revealing the entire ecstatic response is to the most minimal possible physical contact
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's standout line in the first half. Perfect economy (four words), perfect structure (sets up against Mel's ecstatic reaction), and character-specific to both Bret (his genuine disengagement) and Mel (her pathological over-reaction to any contact with the band). The contrast between Mel's 'massage samurai' and Bret's 'I'm just patting her' is a textbook misdirection-and-reveal. Earns a high score because it's doing so much with so little.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm just patting her.

jokes.text: Oh, I feel like I've hired a gigolo. I'm just kidding. I would never hire... I mean, I would, I would, but I wouldn't pay you. I would. I would pay you so much money. I'm just kidding. That's not how I am. That's not what I'm about. I'm joking. I'm not. I am. I'm not.
jokes.setup: Mel is enjoying the massage and makes a joke about hiring a gigolo
jokes.punchline: Her attempt to walk it back becomes an escalating, self-contradicting spiral of confessions and retractions that reveals exactly how she actually feels
jokes.explanation: Mel's uncontrolled spiral of self-incrimination is excellent character comedy — each retraction reveals more than the previous confession. The escalating 'I am. I'm not.' back-and-forth is a perfect comedic structure that has a clear rhythm. Very quotable and highly specific to Mel's character. One of the best Mel moments in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Oh, I feel like I've hired a gigolo. I'm just kidding. I would never hire... I mean, I would, I would, but I wouldn't pay you. I would. I would pay you so much money. I'm joking. I'm not. I am. I'm not.

jokes.text: Bret, you know how you told me you were good at sex? Are you? — That was just 'cause you asked me in front of Sally.
jokes.setup: Mel asks Bret if he's good at sex, referencing a previous claim he made
jokes.punchline: Bret reveals he only claimed to be good at sex because she asked him in front of someone named Sally — implying he lied to save face in a social situation
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Bret's sexual boast was purely social-pressure-driven is a great character moment — it shows his general conflict-avoidance and social awkwardness. The specificity of 'Sally' as the social witness is a nice touch. Also functions as uncomfortable insight into Mel's relationship with the band.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I would. I would pay you so much money. I'm just kidding. That's not how I am. That's not what I'm about. I'm joking. I'm not. I am. I'm not.

jokes.text: Well, you were lying then. — I was exaggerating a little bit. — Lying.
jokes.setup: Bret says his sex-skill claim was an 'exaggeration'
jokes.punchline: Mel's flat correction — 'Lying' — repeated as a beat after each of Bret's deflections
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical exchange — the one-word correction 'Lying' is a good beat. Bret's semantic distinction between lying and exaggerating is a familiar comedic trope but executed cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, it's a story about a prostitute called Richard gere who gets to go out with a "pretty woman" Julia roberts who pays him a lot of money.

jokes.text: Prostitution is a quick way of making money. It is not degrading.
jokes.setup: Jemaine proposes they become prostitutes to solve their financial problems
jokes.punchline: 'It is not degrading' delivered as a flat, sincere reassurance — as if this is a settled matter that simply needs stating
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of 'It is not degrading' as a formal assurance — a statement delivered to preemptively deflect an obvious objection — is very funny in context. The confidence with which Jemaine asserts this is perfectly wrong. Jemaine-specific in its unfounded certainty.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: 'Cause we never get sex or get paid.

jokes.text: The Pretty Woman plot summary — 'It's a story about a prostitute called Richard Gere who gets to go out with a pretty woman Julia Roberts who pays him a lot of money.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine invokes Pretty Woman to argue prostitution is desirable; Bret hasn't seen it
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's summary reverses the roles — he describes Richard Gere as the prostitute and Julia Roberts as the client who pays him, getting the entire plot backwards
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misremembering — Jemaine has the entire film backwards and delivers his wrong summary with complete confidence. The addition of 'do you think Julia Roberts is a pretty woman?' as a rhetorical checkpoint ('Yes') before the pitch is excellent structure. Very quotable and the kind of joke that improves with repetition.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, the ladies go crazy for my sugarlumps.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Your what?

jokes.text: Jemaine, I don't think we're gonna get sex and get paid. — Why not? — 'Cause we never get sex or get paid.
jokes.setup: Jemaine imagines a prostitution scenario where they get sex and money
jokes.punchline: Bret points out the fundamental problem with this plan: their historical failure to obtain either sex or money independently makes both simultaneously unlikely
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. The logic is airtight and devastating: if you can't get sex, and you can't get paid, the combination of the two is also out of reach. Bret's delivery is perfectly deadpan and it functions as a concise thesis statement about the show's entire premise. Highly quotable. The structure of repeating the phrase with 'or' instead of 'and' is a crisp comedic technique.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: let me tell you, I see you girls checking out my trunks

jokes.text: Bret, the ladies go crazy for my sugarlumps.
jokes.setup: Bret expresses doubt about their viability as prostitutes
jokes.punchline: Jemaine confidently asserts that 'the ladies go crazy for my sugarlumps' — 'sugarlumps' being used to refer to his genitals with comically misplaced confidence
jokes.explanation: The setup for the Sugarlumps song — 'sugarlumps' as a term of pride for Jemaine's genitals delivered with total unselfconsciousness is funny on its own. The word choice ('sugarlumps') is inspired: simultaneously absurd, vaguely disgusting, and strangely sweet. Jemaine's unfounded sexual confidence is a perfect character note.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: my dungarees make them hunger-ees

jokes.text: SONG: 'Sugarlumps' — a Beyoncé-style empowerment anthem about Jemaine's genitals, complete with Bret as a backup dancer/vocalist
jokes.setup: Jemaine insists women find him irresistible; the show transitions into a full musical number
jokes.punchline: An elaborate, professionally produced pop/R&B parody in which the 'empowerment anthem' form (clearly riffing on Beyoncé-style songs) is applied entirely to a man's self-aggrandizing ode to his own genitals — with Bret as reluctant hype-man and the pawnshop verse escalating to testicles sold as hacky sacks
jokes.explanation: The Sugarlumps song is the episode's comedic centerpiece and one of the show's most celebrated musical numbers. The genre parody is precise — it perfectly captures the structure and production values of a late-2000s female empowerment pop song while completely inverting the subject matter. The rhyme scheme is elaborate and genuinely clever (pantaloons/hunger-ees/dungarees, 'family jewelry'/'tomfoolery'). The pawnshop verse — where a man sells his testicles for $10 and a frisbee, and they're resold as hacky sacks — is a sudden escalation into dark absurdism that is both unexpected and perfectly logical within the song's internal world. Bret's reluctant participation as hype-man adds a layer of character comedy. This is professional-grade musical comedy writing — the song earns its laughs through craft, not just transgression.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: they're over the moon when I don pantaloons

jokes.text: Hi, do you need a prostitute? — Sorry to interrupt, but do you see my friend over there with the red shirt? He's a bit of a part-time prostitute.
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine attempt to solicit customers on the street
jokes.punchline: Their approach to street solicitation is hilariously polite and apologetic — 'Sorry to interrupt' and 'a bit of a part-time prostitute' treat this as a normal business transaction
jokes.explanation: The Kiwi politeness applied to street solicitation is perfect character comedy. 'Sorry to interrupt' as an opener for prostitute-procurement is a classic Conchords joke — their inability to shed good manners regardless of context. 'A bit of a part-time prostitute' is wonderfully hedged and apologetic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: my cannonballs cause a kerfuffle

jokes.text: Might this entice you? Nut loaf.
jokes.setup: A woman has declined their solicitation offer
jokes.punchline: Jemaine produces a nut loaf as a sweetener for a prostitution offer, as if this is a reasonable incentive
jokes.explanation: Two words: 'Nut loaf.' The specificity of nut loaf — not flowers, not chocolate, not money — as a prostitution incentive is brilliantly absurd. It implies that Jemaine had the nut loaf on him, ready to deploy. The confidence of 'Might this entice you?' before the reveal is perfectly constructed. One of the episode's most quotable moments. Near-perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: ♪ when in walks a guy with his dick in a sling ♪

jokes.text: Bret, perhaps you should wear shorts and show your legs off and maybe wear a cowboy hat and cover up your head a bit.
jokes.setup: Their street solicitation is failing; Jemaine offers marketing advice
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's solution is to show off Bret's legs while covering his face — an implicit acknowledgment that Bret's face is an obstacle to attracting clients
jokes.explanation: A funny piece of advice because of the implied insult — show the legs, hide the face. Jemaine delivering this completely earnestly as marketing strategy is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I said, '10 bucks,' he said, 'no way' '10 bucks and a frisbee,' he said, 'okay'

jokes.text: And also, I think you're gonna have to go the whole way. You can't just hug them. — Just hugs until I get more confident.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is coaching Bret on his prostitution approach
jokes.punchline: Bret's plan is to offer 'just hugs until I get more confident' — treating male prostitution as a skill he needs to build up to gradually
jokes.explanation: 'Just hugs until I get more confident' is a great Bret line — it treats a transgressive scenario with the earnest approach of someone learning a new hobby. The logic of building confidence gradually is both completely wrong-headed and deeply Bret. Quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: and sold 'em as hacky Sacks later that day

jokes.text: The pricing negotiation — Jemaine pitches prices: '20-40-60... Okay 10-30-50... $5. He'll go all the way and he's clean.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is trying to negotiate a price for Bret's services with a passerby
jokes.punchline: Jemaine rapidly caves on his prices, ending at '$5, he'll go all the way and he's clean' — the health assurance 'he's clean' being particularly funny as a desperate final selling point
jokes.explanation: The rapid price collapse from 20 to $5 is well-executed, and 'he's clean' as the ultimate pitch — a health qualification that simultaneously sounds like a dog-grooming ad — is a good joke. The negotiation format is a solid cringe structure.

dialogue.line: You should be the prostitute. You bought the cup.

jokes.text: What expression's on your face? — Um, guilty expression. — What expression's on your face? — Sad because we don't have any electricity, but satisfied 'cause I was right about the cup.
jokes.setup: The flat is dark (electricity cut off); the two sit in darkness
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's compound expression — 'sad because we don't have electricity, but satisfied because I was right about the cup' — is both petty and deeply in character
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback to the episode's central conflict. Jemaine's petty vindication — sitting in darkness but smug about the cup — is entirely in character and wrings new comedy from the episode's central premise. 'Satisfied 'cause I was right about the cup' while living without electricity is a great image. The format of describing their expressions in the dark is an inventive staging choice.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Nut loaf.

jokes.text: The Superstraw economics reveal — it takes five straws at 50 cents each to make one Superstraw sold for $1, resulting in a net loss
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks about the Superstraw business — price, cost of materials
jokes.punchline: Five straws at 50 cents each = $2.50 cost to produce a product sold for $1 — Bret has been running a loss-making business and sold 'quite a few'
jokes.explanation: A perfectly structured callback payoff. The Socratic method Jemaine uses to walk Bret through the math — 'how many straws... what's the total cost... two-fift... oh' — gives Bret the moment of dawning horror and makes the audience feel smart for getting there slightly ahead of him. 'I've sold quite a few of them' landing after the revelation that each sale lost money is a great button. Tight, earned, satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: And also, I think you're gonna have to go the whole way. You can't just hug them.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Just hugs until I get more confident.

jokes.text: Murray's second review: 'Flight of the No-Chords. It was hard to tell if the band was a band or a mime troupe. No stars.'
jokes.setup: Murray reads another review from the newsletter
jokes.punchline: 'No stars' — escalating from 2/100 to a complete zero, and 'mime troupe' as the comparison for a band with no instruments
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 2/100 to 'no stars' is a solid running gag development. 'Mime troupe' is a good comparison. The joke is slightly lessened by Murray again presenting his own review as external criticism, though Jemaine calls this out.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 20-40-60. Okay, 10-30-50. $5. He'll go all the way and he's clean.

jokes.text: It doesn't matter who wrote it, Jemaine. This is what people are reading. This is the written report on you that someone's written. — Yeah. You.
jokes.setup: Jemaine points out Murray wrote the review himself
jokes.punchline: Murray insists the authorship is irrelevant because it's what 'people are reading' — 'the written report on you that someone's written' — Jemaine's flat 'Yeah. You.' deflates this
jokes.explanation: 'The written report on you that someone's written' is a wonderfully redundant Murray-ism, and Jemaine's deadpan 'Yeah. You.' is the perfect counter. Murray's insistence on the objectivity of his own criticism is a good character joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Sad because we don't have any electricity, but satisfied 'cause I was right about the cup.

jokes.text: Can you think of any successful musicians with no instruments? — The Mormon Tabernacle Choir. — Boyz II Men. — Bobby McFerrin. — Pavarotti. — Bret's right. There are none.
jokes.setup: Murray challenges them to name successful acts with no instruments
jokes.punchline: Bret and Jemaine name four famous a cappella/vocal acts; Murray declares 'Bret's right. There are none.' — dismissing all four examples as non-existent
jokes.explanation: A well-structured list joke with a great button. The guys give genuinely good answers (all famous vocal acts), and Murray's dismissal of all of them as evidence that 'there are none' is perfect managerial obliviousness. 'Bret's right' — attributing the conclusion to Bret who said none of those words — is a nice additional absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How many straws does it take to make a superstraw?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Five.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What's the total cost of those five straws?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Two-fift... oh.

jokes.text: The timeline visual gag — Murray's 14-year guitar recovery timeline where the scale 'got out of hand at the start'
jokes.setup: Murray has made a timeline to show how long it will take to raise money for the guitars
jokes.punchline: The timeline is enormous and goes to 14 years, and Murray explains the scale 'got out of hand at the start' — implying the chart is physically large due to an error in scaling that was too late to fix
jokes.explanation: Murray's bureaucratic visual aids are a recurring character trait, and the 14-year timeline that 'got out of hand' is a great physical prop joke (inferred from context). 'You should see the first one I made — it's definitely an improvement' implies an even more disastrous original version. Pure Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Flight of the no-chords. It was hard to tell if the band was a band or a mime troop. No stars.

jokes.text: You should see the first one I made, though. It's definitely an improvement.
jokes.setup: Murray's timeline is already absurdly large and poorly scaled
jokes.punchline: There's an earlier, worse version — implying the current disaster of a chart is the improved edition
jokes.explanation: A good button on the timeline joke. The implication of a worse previous version — never shown — is funnier than showing it would be. Economy of suggestion.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: The Mormon tabernacle choir. Boyz ii men. Bobby mcferrin. Pavarotti.

jokes.text: The consulate gig offer — Murray calls Bret's existing job at Eddie's as a 'gig' and tries to take commission
jokes.setup: Murray announces he's got Bret a paid gig — it turns out to be Bret's existing day job
jokes.punchline: Murray tries to take a management commission on Bret's already-existing job, and when told he can't, solemnly notes it down: 'No commission on Bret's job'
jokes.explanation: Murray's delusional management authority is perfectly demonstrated here — he genuinely believes he can claim commission on an existing job he had nothing to do with. Writing it down formally as a policy note ('No commission on Bret's job') is excellent character business. His seriousness about the documentation is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Basically, you should have your guitars back in about 14 years.

jokes.text: I could work on that with you and get a cut of it. — You don't want to. — I'd like to. — No.
jokes.setup: Murray asks about Jemaine's 'outside-source income' (prostitution, though Murray doesn't know what it is yet)
jokes.punchline: Jemaine knows Murray absolutely does not want to be involved and refuses to say more; Murray's eagerness to get a cut of something he doesn't understand is perfect
jokes.explanation: A short, efficient exchange. Jemaine's protective 'No' — knowing Murray would be appalled — is good comedy. The implication of the unspoken context (prostitution) makes the brevity funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No commission on Bret's job.

jokes.text: Jemaine calls Carol to retroactively charge for past sex — 'do you remember how we slept together about six times I think it was last year in February? Um, so I was wondering if I might be able to get some money for that, maybe about $40 a time? That's $3 off.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine decides to pursue prostitution; calls an ex-partner
jokes.punchline: He retroactively invoices a past sexual partner, offering a volume discount of '$3 off' per encounter, and then asks her out
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and among the finest character comedy in the series. The retroactive invoice is a concept so specific and so wrong that it's brilliant — Jemaine has literally called someone to charge them for sex that already happened, offering what he presents as a generous discount ('$3 off'). The '$3 off' detail is perfectly chosen: it's a discount so small it's insulting. Then, having tried to charge her, he immediately pivots to asking her out. The complete tonal confidence with which Jemaine delivers this is impeccable. It's original, tightly structured, deeply character-specific, and earns every laugh through pure internal logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I was just calling to say... do you remember how we slept together about six times I think it was last year in february? Uh, yeah. I'm a prostitute now.

jokes.text: Free condoms from the human condom! [Jemaine dressed as a condom handing out free condoms on the street]
jokes.setup: Jemaine has decided to pursue prostitution; next scene shows him on the street
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is dressed as a giant condom and handing out free condoms as a marketing strategy for his prostitution services
jokes.explanation: A strong visual gag — the condom costume as both product demonstration and advertising for a would-be gigolo. The specificity of the costume being a literal human-sized condom is committed and funny. The line 'Free condoms from the human condom!' is a good slogan.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Also, would you be interested in going out sometime?

jokes.text: Mel sees Jemaine's lobby ad — 'I saw it on the advertisement on the lobby notice board. Did you take that off the lobby notice board? — Yes, and I don't have my own copy.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is surprised Mel knows about his prostitution
jokes.punchline: Mel saw his ad on the lobby notice board — and when Jemaine asks if she took it down, she confirms it AND that she's kept it, having no personal copy
jokes.explanation: Mel's possessiveness of Jemaine's prostitution advertisement — removing it from the lobby so no one else can see it and keeping it — is perfect Mel characterization. 'I don't have my own copy' implies she'd want one. Entirely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Just a normal book... nondescript. Just a book I had.
What book?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Just a super... a book that tells you where to sell your body.

jokes.text: Try hotels... fancy ones and ones near the airport. — Oh, okay. — How did you know that? — I read it in a book. — What book? — Just a normal book.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is struggling to find clients; Mel offers professional advice about where to find them
jokes.punchline: Mel knows exactly where male prostitutes find clients (fancy hotels, airport hotels) from 'just a normal book' — an increasingly elaborate and suspicious characterization of the book's normalcy
jokes.explanation: The escalating defensiveness about the 'just a normal book' is excellent — each qualifier ('just a normal book, nondescript... just a book I had... just a very normal book... just a super...') makes it more suspicious. The final revelation — 'a book that tells you where to sell your body' — followed by 'just a normal book with all kinds of facts, that's one of 'em' is a perfect button. Very Mel.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Can you imagine it? Out of the hundreds of people that use the internet, only I was willing to help him.

jokes.text: 'What was the title?' — 'Just How to Get It Done.'
jokes.setup: Mel has been defensively describing her book about where to solicit prostitution clients as 'just a normal book'
jokes.punchline: The title — 'Just How to Get It Done' — is deliberately vague and corporate-sounding, a perfect euphemism for a book about hiring prostitutes
jokes.explanation: The button on the book escalation — a title that sounds like a motivational business guide but is clearly a guide to hiring sex workers. The vagueness is perfectly chosen. 'Just How to Get It Done' is maximally ambiguous and the understated humor of Mel presenting it as a general-purpose book is the joke's engine.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Why aren't I a prostitute? Good one, nigel. Why isn't nigel a prostitute? We could all be in a prostitute club.

jokes.text: This lift is so slow.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been caught soliciting prostitution outside Mel's building; they are now in her elevator together
jokes.punchline: Mel breaks an extremely uncomfortable silence with a completely mundane observation about elevator speed — the most neutral possible deflection from the situation
jokes.explanation: The small talk after catching your obsessive fan crush prostituting himself outside your building is a precise, recognizable social beat. The banality is the joke — it's a very human response to an extremely abnormal situation.

dialogue.speaker: Nigel
dialogue.line: Nagoota tata lacie.

jokes.text: He's out working the beat. — Drumming? — No, he's a gigolo. — A what? — He's a prostitute. — Jemaine's a hooker. — A whore? Is he? — A prostitute. — A male prostitute.
jokes.setup: Murray asks where Jemaine is
jokes.punchline: The escalating synonyms — gigolo → prostitute → hooker → whore → prostitute → male prostitute — each more frank than the last, as Murray processes what Jemaine is doing
jokes.explanation: A well-paced escalation through synonyms — Murray's dawning comprehension is charted through increasingly plain language. 'A whore? Is he?' delivered with genuine shock and the final clarifying 'a male prostitute' (as if the regular noun wasn't clear enough) are good beats.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There's the keyboard here. There's the mouse. You can just go up and down on the keys. I don't use that. There's the TV screen.

jokes.text: Can you imagine it? Out of the hundreds of people that use the internet, only I was willing to help him.
jokes.setup: Murray introduces Nigel Soladu and explains their connection
jokes.punchline: Murray frames being the only person to fall for a (seemingly genuine) internet solicitation as evidence of his unique generosity and open-mindedness — 'only I was willing to help him'
jokes.explanation: Murray's reframing of gullibility as moral superiority — 'only I was willing to help him' — is perfect. It retroactively also explains why he's susceptible to scams: he thinks it makes him a better person. The 'hundreds of people that use the internet' is a great additional absurdity (vastly underestimating internet usage).

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: you can say no to being a night-look, a boy hook, a red boy bro-ho

jokes.text: Bret, you've got to get out there and stop Jemaine. He shouldn't be selling himself to the street!
jokes.setup: Murray learns Jemaine is a prostitute
jokes.punchline: 'Selling himself to the street' — Murray's moral outrage is expressed in a slightly wrong phrase (usually 'selling himself on the street') that gives it a vaguely corporate flavor
jokes.explanation: A decent Murray-ism — 'selling himself to the street' has a slightly wrong quality that's typical of his slightly-off formal speech patterns. Not the sharpest Murray line but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: do you have any other skills... like typing?

jokes.text: Why aren't I a prostitute? Why aren't I a prostitute, Bret? Good one, Nigel.
jokes.setup: Murray is outraged about Jemaine's prostitution; the conversation spirals
jokes.punchline: Murray appears to briefly consider his own eligibility for prostitution, then credits Nigel with some observation ('Good one, Nigel') that is never explained
jokes.explanation: Murray briefly entertaining his own viability as a prostitute before complimenting Nigel for an unheard comment is good absurdist Murray business. The unexplained 'Good one, Nigel' is funny because Nigel has said nothing we've heard.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I have a few rules. No laughing, especially during, okay? It puts me off.

jokes.text: We could all be in a prostitute club. — It's 'cause of that cup. You should never have bought that cup.
jokes.setup: The conversation about prostitution spirals out of control
jokes.punchline: Murray's 'prostitute club' concept is immediately deflated by the callback to the cup — which Murray pinpoints as the ultimate cause of everything
jokes.explanation: The cup callback landing in the middle of this chaotic scene is a good structural moment — the episode's central premise keeps being returned to as the root cause. Murray's insistence that the cup is responsible for Jemaine's prostitution is absurd but also literally true within the episode's logic.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No no, I'm just the guy that wears the big condom.

jokes.text: Also, I've got something to say. Nagoota tata lacie. / Did I say that right? / I don't know what you said. / I'm still trying on that one.
jokes.setup: Murray wants to say something in what he presumably thinks is Nigel's language.
jokes.punchline: Murray delivers an entirely made-up string of syllables ('Nagoota tata lacie'), asks if he said it right, and the native speaker has no idea what he said. Murray has invented a foreign language.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Murray bit. The attempt at cultural courtesy completely fabricated — not misremembered but entirely invented — and delivered with pride is perfectly Murray. The punchline 'I'm still trying on that one' implies he's been practicing this meaningless phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I knew if you bought a cup, we'd end up in jail. I knew it.

jokes.text: I just wanted a new cup. We only had one cup. So there's two of us, I often have to wait for Jemaine to finish his... anyway.
jokes.setup: Murray accuses Bret of causing everything with the cup purchase
jokes.punchline: Bret's defense trails off awkwardly — 'I often have to wait for Jemaine to finish his... anyway' — implying he was about to overshare something about Jemaine's cup usage
jokes.explanation: The trailing off — 'I often have to wait for Jemaine to finish his... anyway' — is a great implied joke. The 'anyway' signals a decision not to finish a thought that the audience can supply themselves. Good economy and a nice callback to the cup roster specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Jemaine, was it a good thing to prostitute yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well...

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: no, it wasn't.

jokes.text: Nagoota tata lacie. — Did I say that right? — I don't know what you said.
jokes.setup: Murray wants to thank Nigel in his native language, having apparently attempted to learn a phrase
jokes.punchline: Nigel confirms he doesn't know what Murray said — the phrase is unrecognizable even to its supposed speaker
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical joke. Murray's attempt at a Nigerian phrase is so garbled that even the Nigerian person can't identify it. The exchange is short and lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We were at square two, but now we're back.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: When was that?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Let's stop talking about squares.

jokes.text: Murray's computer instruction — 'There's the keyboard here. There's the mouse. You can just go up and down on the keys. I don't use that. There's the TV screen.'
jokes.setup: Nigel asks to use Murray's computer to check email
jokes.punchline: Murray gives a laborious explanation of basic computer components — 'you can just go up and down on the keys, I don't use that' — revealing his own limited computer use while presenting it as a tutorial
jokes.explanation: Murray explaining that the monitor is a 'TV screen' and that he doesn't use the mouse is a good character moment — his tech incompetence is consistent with his analog, bureaucratic character. 'I don't use that' about the mouse is the best detail.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're like a couple of... jailhouse turkeys.

jokes.text: SONG: 'You Don't Have to Be a Prostitute' — Bret sings a power ballad to rescue Jemaine from prostitution, with practical domestic advice embedded in the verses
jokes.setup: Bret goes to find Jemaine working the streets; the show transitions into a musical number
jokes.punchline: The earnest gospel/power ballad format is applied to the mundane task of talking a friend out of street prostitution; the lyrics include extremely practical domestic advice like 'do you have any other skills, like typing?' and 'you have a roommate, Jemaine, don't bring them home'
jokes.explanation: The second musical set piece and arguably the better of the two for pure joke density per verse. The genre — earnest inspirational ballad, in the vein of 'Wind Beneath My Wings' or a gospel rescue anthem — is applied to the specific and mundane situation of talking a friend off a literal street corner. The genius is in the intrusion of practical concerns into the swelling emotional format: 'do you have any other skills, like typing?' punctures the grandeur perfectly. 'You have a roommate, Jemaine, don't bring them home' is character-specific advice delivered in the mode of spiritual counsel. The dual vocal structure — Bret narrating in third person while Jemaine responds in first person — creates a beautiful formal tension. The music works on its own terms as a genuine parody. Among the episode's two or three best moments.

jokes.text: Jemaine's prostitution client rules: No laughing, especially during; overtime charges after 20 minutes; client shower policy
jokes.setup: A woman offers Jemaine $200; he agrees to take the job
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has an elaborate professional code of conduct: no laughing (especially during), overtime rates, mandatory client shower — all delivered with the gravitas of a serious professional
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's professional policies are perfectly conceived — 'no laughing, especially during, okay? It puts me off' is the standout line, a vulnerability framed as a professional requirement. The overtime clause and mandatory shower add escalating layers of misplaced formality. The 'especially during' qualifier on the no-laughing rule is the best detail. Deeply Jemaine.

jokes.text: Number two: If you go overtime, I'm gonna have to charge you for the next 20 minutes. One or two minutes is... / Do you want to do this or not? / Uh, yes I do.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is listing his professional terms.
jokes.punchline: His overtime policy is presented as a standard business terms clause — and when the client interrupts, his professional dignity wavers immediately: 'Uh, yes I do.'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat. The power dynamic inversion — Jemaine establishing terms confidently, then immediately caving when questioned — is character-consistent and funny.

jokes.text: No laughing, especially during, okay? It puts me off.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is listing his professional rules for the prostitution client
jokes.punchline: 'Especially during' — the timing specification reveals this is a real vulnerability Jemaine has experienced, and 'it puts me off' frames it as a practical professional concern
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best individual line and one of the best jokes in the series. 'No laughing, especially during, okay? It puts me off.' In ten words, it: (1) implies a vulnerable past experience; (2) frames that vulnerability as a professional policy; (3) uses 'especially during' as a perfect temporal qualifier that implies the laughing is understandable AROUND the event but not DURING it; (4) 'it puts me off' is the most functional, unemotional way to express deep personal hurt. The economy is near-perfect. It is entirely Jemaine — his combination of unearned confidence and hidden fragility is the character in miniature. Calibrated against the anchor examples: this is comparable to the 'Snip, snap!' level of quotability and character specificity.

jokes.text: The client has a shower — [Bret arrives to rescue Jemaine mid-appointment]
jokes.setup: Jemaine has told his client she needs to shower; she has gone to comply
jokes.punchline: Bret arrives to rescue Jemaine precisely as the client is in the shower — the rescue operation is made physically awkward by the situation
jokes.explanation: The timing of the rescue relative to the shower is a decent physical comedy setup, though the transcript doesn't give enough detail to score this highly. Functions as a transitional cringe moment.

jokes.text: Get out of here. — No, I'm serious. It's totally fine. — Murray said it's degrading as well. — Oh, it isn't degrading. It's fine. — Are you sure? — Yeah yeah yeah. It is more social.
jokes.setup: Bret arrives to rescue Jemaine from prostitution; tells him the money situation is resolved
jokes.punchline: Jemaine, now committed to prostitution, argues it's 'more social' — having apparently come around entirely on the 'degrading' question he himself raised
jokes.explanation: The reversal — Jemaine now defending prostitution as 'more social' just as he's being rescued from it — is good character work. His specific defense ('you can sit down, have a cup of tea together') is nicely mundane.

jokes.text: It is more social. — Yeah, exactly. — You can sit down, have a cup of tea together.
jokes.setup: Bret feels bad about the cup causing all this; Jemaine is being generous about it
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's silver lining for having two cups — you can now have tea simultaneously with a prostitution client — returns to the episode's first premise
jokes.explanation: The final cup callback — the cup is now justified because you can have tea with your prostitution client — closes the episode's comedic loop perfectly. The absurd logic is internally consistent: Bret's original need (simultaneous tea) is now served by a situation caused by the cup's purchase. Excellent structural callback with strong economy.

jokes.text: It is more social. — Yeah, exactly. — You can sit down, have a cup of tea together.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is defending prostitution as a lifestyle choice
jokes.punchline: 'You can sit down, have a cup of tea together' — his example of prostitution's social benefits invokes the episode's central cup motif
jokes.explanation: A beautiful callback. The cup motif that opened the episode — the source of all their financial catastrophe — resurfaces here as Jemaine's specific example of what makes prostitution appealing: the opportunity for a shared cup of tea. The circularity is elegant and entirely earned. It's a funny line on its own ('you can sit down, have a cup of tea together' as a selling point for prostitution) that becomes significantly funnier with the callback context.

jokes.text: Get out, Bret. I've got a customer. It's a pretty woman.
jokes.setup: Bret is trying to extract Jemaine; Jemaine refuses to leave
jokes.punchline: 'It's a pretty woman' — a callback to Jemaine's earlier Pretty Woman pitch, implying his reference film has come true for him
jokes.explanation: A great callback to the botched Pretty Woman summary — Jemaine genuinely believes his scenario has now played out. 'It's a pretty woman' delivered with quiet pride is a perfect button. Economy is high.

jokes.text: Police. Open up. — I called them to come and save you.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has his first client and is being philosophical about the cup; police suddenly knock
jokes.punchline: Bret called the police — ostensibly to rescue Jemaine — which results in both of them being arrested
jokes.explanation: A functional plot escalation joke — Bret's well-intentioned rescue backfires completely. The irony is clear but it's a fairly standard sitcom complication. Works as a scene button.

jokes.text: Police arrive — 'You, are you the prostitute?' — 'Yes, ma'am.' — 'You're a prostitute as well?' — 'No no, I'm just the guy that wears the big condom.'
jokes.setup: Police arrive responding to a solicitation call (which Bret made to 'save' Jemaine)
jokes.punchline: Bret's explanation for his presence — 'I'm just the guy that wears the big condom' — is both true (condom costume) and completely incriminating-sounding in context
jokes.explanation: A textbook misdirection-and-reveal payoff. 'I'm just the guy that wears the big condom' is an objectively true statement (he wore the condom costume) that sounds maximally guilty in a police interrogation. It's also a great callback to the earlier condom costume visual gag. The line is quotable, perfectly constructed, and earns its laugh through setup payoff across the episode.

jokes.text: A bit boring in jail, eh? — There's nothing to do.
jokes.setup: They've been arrested and are in jail
jokes.punchline: Their assessment of jail is that it's 'a bit boring' — the understatement of describing incarceration as merely unentertaining
jokes.explanation: The Kiwi understatement applied to incarceration is a clean character joke. 'A bit boring' for jail is perfectly calibrated — neither panicked nor angry, just mildly inconvenienced. 'There's nothing to do' as the confirming response is a good deadpan button.

jokes.text: I knew if you bought a cup, we'd end up in jail. I knew it.
jokes.setup: They are sitting in jail
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's in-jail vindication speech — retroactively attributing their incarceration to the cup purchase — is delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic callback in its purest form. 'I knew if you bought a cup, we'd end up in jail. I knew it.' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode and perfectly encapsulates the show's central premise (absurd cause and effect) and Jemaine's character (petty, vindicated, completely right by accident). The callback to the cold open from a jail cell gives the episode structural elegance.

jokes.text: We were at square two, but now we're back. — When was that? — Let's stop talking about squares.
jokes.setup: Murray declares they're 'back at square one' after all the money is spent on bail
jokes.punchline: 'We were at square two, but now we're back' — Murray's assertion that they briefly attained 'square two' is questioned; he can't defend it and ends the conversation
jokes.explanation: 'When was that?' forcing Murray to abandon the metaphor entirely — 'Let's stop talking about squares' — is a great escalation and reversal. Murray's inability to support his own idiom is perfectly in character. 'Let's stop talking about squares' is quotable as a non-sequitur.

jokes.text: You're like a couple of... jailhouse turkeys.
jokes.setup: Murray is berating Bret and Jemaine after bailing them out of jail
jokes.punchline: 'Jailhouse turkeys' — a completely invented insult that doesn't quite make sense but is delivered with total authority
jokes.explanation: 'Jailhouse turkeys' is a wonderfully nonsensical Murray insult — it riffs on 'jailbird' but replaces it with a less threatening bird, giving it a deflationary quality. Murray's invented vocabulary is a recurring character trait. Quotable in its absurdity.

jokes.text: You're like a couple of... jailhouse turkeys.
jokes.setup: Murray is delivering his exit lecture after bailing them out
jokes.punchline: 'Jailhouse turkeys' as Murray's chosen insult — a phrase he seems to have coined on the spot that means nothing and everything simultaneously
jokes.explanation: 'Jailhouse turkeys' is a Murray-specific insult that sounds authoritative while meaning nothing specific. It's the kind of phrase only this character would deploy — slightly formal, slightly absurd, delivered with full managerial weight. Quotable precisely because it's meaningless.

jokes.text: When's the electricity coming back on? — They said it would be on by now. [lights turn on] — You'll be pleased about that.
jokes.setup: They've been in the dark apartment throughout; Bret asks about electricity
jokes.punchline: The lights come on at exactly that moment; Jemaine's dry 'you'll be pleased about that' is an understatement of the actual positive development
jokes.explanation: A clean structural callback — the electricity that was cut off due to the cup finally returns. 'You'll be pleased about that' as the response to actual good news is typical Jemaine understatement. Works as a tidy episode resolution.

jokes.text: The electricity comes back on — Jemaine's reaction shot to Bret: 'You'll be pleased about that.'
jokes.setup: The electricity has been out all episode; it suddenly returns
jokes.punchline: Jemaine points out to Bret that the lights are back on — phrased as 'you'll be pleased about that' — as if Bret needs notification and mild grudging acknowledgment is the appropriate register
jokes.explanation: A quiet callback that closes the electricity thread. Jemaine's phrasing — 'you'll be pleased about that' — implies he is only grudgingly acknowledging this as a positive, given the cup was the root cause. Understated closure.

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine s fortunes unravel after Bret buys a new tea cup; Jemaine goes to desperate measures to supplement his income.
